metadata.title: Proof of Concept at ANY Stage (Slides 8–9)
metadata.playlist: Sell the Vision — Your Investor Pitch Video
metadata.source: INFO 7375 Assignment 7 brief — Video Pitch to Investors: Slides 8–9 (dev plan + proof of concept)

beats.narration_text: Hola — Liam, Episode 5 of 7. Slides 8 and 9 are the part most students dread because they are afraid to show where they actually are. They apologize for not having finished code. They pad out a concept with vague milestones. Or they overclaim — 'our prototype is nearly ready' when it isn't. All three are wrong. Slides 8 and 9 have one job: show honestly where you are and make it compelling. Investors fund ideas as often as they fund products. The stage does not disqualify you — hiding the stage does. This episode covers slide 8, the development plan, and slide 9, the proof of concept. Both slides are different for a student at concept stage, mockup stage, prototype stage, and MVP stage. All four versions are valid pitches.

props.segment: Proof of Concept at ANY Stage (Slides 8–9)
props.command: Frame my honest current stage as a strength — then draft slide 8 (dev plan with milestones) and slide 9 (POC evidence matched to my stage).
props.runningText: the stage doesn't disqualify you — hiding the stage does. Concept, mockup, prototype, MVP — all valid pitches…
output: Slide 8: current status (concept/mockup/prototype/MVP) + 4–6 milestones with timeline
Slide 9: evidence matched to stage — working code / mockups / wireframes / architecture diagram
Transparency play: 'investors fund ideas' — sell what you have, don't apologize

beats.narration_text: Slide 8 is the development plan. Its job is to answer: where are you now, and what is the path from here to a working product? Start with your current status — one of four honest labels. Concept: you have a clear idea and an architecture but no working code. Mockup: you have wireframes or a Figma prototype that demonstrates the user journey. Prototype: you have working code for some or most of the core features, but it is not production-ready. MVP: you have a minimal working version users can actually run. Pick the honest label. Then write four to six milestones with a timeline. Each milestone is specific and achievable: 'Week 1 to 2: data ingestion pipeline. Week 3 to 4: AI processing node. Week 5 to 6: output formatting. Week 7: user testing with 3 email marketing coordinators. Week 8: submit to Canvas.' That is a dev plan. What a dev plan is not: 'we will build the full product and launch to users.' That is not a milestone — it is a goal. Milestones are checkpoints, not destinations.

artifactLines: 4 honest stages: Concept (idea + architecture) · Mockup (wireframes/Figma) · Prototype (partial working code) · MVP (users can run it).
Pick the honest label. Then write 4–6 milestones with a weekly timeline.
Example: 'Wk 1–2: ingestion pipeline · Wk 3–4: AI node · Wk 5–6: output format · Wk 7: user test.'
A milestone is a checkpoint, not a destination. 'Build the full product' is not a milestone.
Be specific and achievable — investors are grading your ability to plan, not just your progress.
props.sparkLine: Milestones are checkpoints. Pick the honest stage. Then show the path.

beats.act: slide 9 — proof of concept, three versions
beats.narration_text: Slide 9 is the proof of concept slide. Its job is to show evidence — whatever evidence you have — that this is buildable. There are three versions depending on your stage. If you have working code: demo it. One 60-second screen recording showing one complete real output. Not a tour of the interface — a real input going in and a real output coming out. If you have mockups — a Figma prototype or a set of wireframes — walk the user journey as if it is real. 'Here is what the email coordinator sees at 8am. She opens the tool, clicks run, and three minutes later this report lands in her inbox.' Narrate it with confidence. If you are at concept stage, your POC is the architecture. The n8n workflow diagram from your A5B work, the data flow from source to output, the phase gates you designed. Present it as: 'We have validated the architecture. This is the path the data takes.' A well-documented concept is not an apology. It is evidence of disciplined thinking — which is what investors are actually evaluating at the early stage.

artifactLines: Working code: 60-sec screen recording — real input in, real output out. Not a UI tour.
Mockups/Figma: walk the user journey as if it's real. 'She opens the tool, clicks run, report lands.'
Concept/architecture: n8n workflow diagram + data flow + phase gates = validated architecture.
A well-documented concept is not an apology — it's evidence of disciplined thinking.
Investors at early stage are evaluating your ability to think through a problem, not just your code.
props.sparkLine: Show the evidence you have. A validated architecture is evidence.

beats.act: the transparency play — sell what you have
beats.narration_text: The transparency play is one of the most underused strengths in a student pitch. Here is how it works. Instead of apologizing for where you are, you acknowledge it as a deliberate decision. 'We are at the concept stage with a validated architecture and a clear path to an MVP. We chose to build the brief before the code because a well-defined product is faster to build than an under-defined one.' That framing turns transparency into discipline. Investors see founders all the time who over-claimed and under-delivered. A student who can say clearly where they are and why, and show a realistic path forward, often lands more credibly than a student who has running code but cannot explain the business case. The thing to avoid: apologizing. 'I know this is just a concept' or 'we haven't gotten to building it yet' — these are apologies, not framings. Every stage has a strength: concept has clarity, mockups have user insight, prototype has technical validation, MVP has user feedback. Lead with the strength.

props.artifactHeading: Sell what you have — don't apologize for it
artifactLines: Frame your stage as a strength, not a limitation.
Example: 'We're at concept stage with validated architecture — we built the brief before the code.'
Every stage has a strength: concept = clarity · mockup = user insight · prototype = validation · MVP = feedback.
Avoid: 'I know this is just a concept' — that's an apology, not a framing.
Investors fund ideas. A student who explains their stage clearly often lands more credibly than one who overclaims.
props.sparkLine: Transparency is a strength. Apologizing is a weakness. Lead with the strength.

beats.narration_text: The traps that kill slides 8 and 9. First: vague milestones. 'Build the product' and 'launch to users' are goals, not milestones. A milestone is a specific, observable checkpoint — something you can either mark done or not done. Write milestones that pass that test. Second: overclaiming the stage. If you say 'our prototype is nearly ready' and the grader asks to see it and there is nothing, that is worse than saying 'we are at concept stage.' The honest label is always the right label. Third: apologizing for no code. 'I know we don't have a working product yet' is the first thing some students say on slide 8. Do not do this. Say your stage and your milestones with the same confidence you would use to say 'we have a working MVP.' Confidence in your plan is confidence in your product. Fourth: showing a UI tour instead of a real output. If you have working code, the most compelling thing on slide 9 is a real output — a real summary, a real report, a real recommendation that the tool generated. Show what the tool actually produces.

artifactLines: Trap 1: vague milestones. 'Build the product' is not a milestone — it's a goal. Make it observable/done-or-not.
Trap 2: overclaiming the stage. The honest label is always right. Over-claimed + under-delivered = credibility loss.
Trap 3: apologizing for no code. Say your stage with the same confidence as 'we have an MVP.'
Trap 4: UI tour instead of real output. If you have code, show a real input → real output on slide 9.
Confidence in the plan = confidence in the product. No apologies.
props.sparkLine: Say your stage with confidence. Confidence in the plan is confidence in the product.

beats.narration_text: Verdict for Episode 5. Slide 8: one honest stage label, four to six specific milestones, a timeline. Milestones are checkpoints — done or not done. Not goals. Slide 9: evidence matched to your stage. Working code shows a real output. Mockups walk the user journey as if real. Concept stage presents the validated architecture. All three versions are legitimate investor presentations. The transparency play: acknowledge your stage with confidence, frame it as a deliberate decision, lead with the strength of that stage. Do not apologize. Do not overclaim. The only wrong answer is hiding your stage — because it comes out in the Q&A and it kills credibility. Say what it is. Make it compelling. That is slides 8 and 9.

props.artifactTitle: Proof of Concept at ANY Stage
artifactLines: Slide 8: honest stage label + 4–6 specific milestone checkpoints + timeline.
Slide 9: evidence matched to stage — real output / user journey / validated architecture.
All four stages (concept, mockup, prototype, MVP) are legitimate investor presentations.
Transparency play: acknowledge stage with confidence · frame it · lead with its strength.
Do not apologize. Do not overclaim. Hiding your stage comes out in Q&A.
Say what it is. Make it compelling.

beats.narration_text: Your turn. Paste this prompt into Claude. Fill in the brackets before running. The prompt reads: Here is my Madison project and current stage: [paste your honest stage label — concept, mockup, prototype, or MVP — and a 2-to-3 sentence description of what you have built so far]. Write Slides 8 and 9. Slide 8: write the development plan. Use the honest stage label. Write 5 specific milestones with a weekly timeline from now to the assignment deadline. Each milestone should be specific enough to be marked done or not done. Slide 9: write the proof of concept section matched to my stage. If concept stage: present the architecture as a validated design. If mockup: walk the user journey in 3 to 5 steps. If prototype or MVP: write a script for a 60-second demo that shows one real input going in and one real output coming out. For all stages: frame the current stage as a deliberate decision, not an apology. Lead with the strength of that stage. After the prompt runs: are the milestones specific enough to be marked done? Is the POC framed with confidence? If you wrote an apology anywhere on either slide, delete it.

props.segment: Proof of Concept at ANY Stage (Slides 8–9)
props.command: Here is my project and stage: [paste honest stage label + 2–3 sentence description of what you have]. Write Slides 8–9. S8: honest stage label + 5 milestones with weekly timeline (each specific enough to mark done/not done). S9: POC matched to stage — architecture (concept) / 3–5 step user journey (mockup) / 60-sec demo script for real input→output (prototype/MVP). Frame the stage as a deliberate decision, not an apology.
props.runningText: paste your honest stage and what you have built so far — the transparency play only works when you commit to the honest label…

beats.narration_text: Proof of Concept at ANY Stage. Liam, in for Bear.
